era_context: Classical Greece continues its post-Persian Wars consolidation. Athens is rising as a naval power under Cimon's leadership. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is beginning to face challenges to its hegemony. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is traditionally dated to end in 476 BCE, marking the transition to the Warring States period, though this is a historiographic convention rather than a discrete event. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I continues to dominate the Near East and western Asia.

events.title: Athenian naval operations and consolidation of Delian League power
events.description: Cimon continues Athenian naval campaigns against Persian-held territories and pirate strongholds. The Delian League's military operations are now well-established, with Athens providing the dominant naval force. These campaigns extend Athenian influence and extract tribute from allied states, gradually transforming the league into an Athenian-dominated empire. The year 476 BCE marks no specific major battle or event, but rather the continuation of a process that began in the 480s BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98–99)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Athenian expansion is well-attested, but specific events for 476 BCE are not clearly distinguished in sources. The year is assigned by convention.

events.title: Traditional end of the Spring and Autumn period in China; transition to Warring States period
events.description: Chinese historiography traditionally marks 476 BCE as the end of the Spring and Autumn period (Chunqiu, 770–476 BCE) and the beginning of the Warring States period (Zhanguo, 475–221 BCE). This division is based on the Chunqiu chronicle and later interpretations. The transition reflects a shift from a nominal Zhou dynasty with regional lords to a system of openly competing, increasingly powerful states. Warfare becomes more frequent, armies larger, and political organization more centralized. However, this is a historiographic convention; the actual transition was gradual, occurring over decades.
key_figures: Confucius (philosopher, lived 551–479 BCE, died during this period)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The date 476 BCE is a historiographic convention, not based on a specific datable event. The Chunqiu ends in 479 BCE (some versions 476 BCE), and later historians used this as a dividing line. The actual transition from Spring and Autumn to Warring States was gradual and cannot be pinpointed to a single year.

events.title: Death of Confucius (traditional date: 479 BCE, alternative dating)
events.description: Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551–479 BCE) is traditionally said to have died in 479 BCE, though some sources place his death in 478 BCE or 476 BCE. His death marks the end of an era in Chinese intellectual history. Confucius was a teacher and philosopher who emphasized ritual propriety (li), filial piety, and moral cultivation. Though he had limited political influence during his lifetime, his teachings became foundational to Chinese civilization. His death occurs during the transition from the Spring and Autumn to the Warring States period, a time of increasing chaos that his philosophy sought to address.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Lunyu (Analects of Confucius), compiled by disciples
Zuozhuan (Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals)

events.certainty_note: Confucius's birth year (551 BCE) is relatively well-attested, but his death year is given as 479 BCE in most sources, with some variation (478 or 476 BCE). The Shiji was compiled ~400 years after his death. No contemporary records of his death survive. The year 479 BCE is the traditional date, but 476 BCE is sometimes cited.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Confucius's death is disputed. Most sources give 479 BCE, but some texts cite 478 or 476 BCE. The Shiji, the most authoritative source, states 479 BCE, but this was compiled 400 years after the fact. The 'end' of the Spring and Autumn period at 476 BCE is a historiographic convention, not a historical event; the transition to the Warring States period was gradual. Some modern scholars argue that the division between these periods is artificial and that the Warring States period actually began earlier, in the 5th century BCE, or later, in the 4th century BCE. The significance attributed to 476 BCE as a turning point in Chinese history is largely a product of later historiographic tradition, not contemporary evidence.
historiographic_note: For Greece, sources are secondary and written decades to centuries after events. For China, the Shiji is the primary source but was compiled 400 years after the Spring and Autumn period. The Chunqiu itself is a sparse chronicle, often cryptic, and later commentaries (Zuozhuan, Gongyang zhuan, Guliang zhuan) provide interpretations that may not reflect original intent. The dating of Confucius's life and death relies on these later sources and is not independently verifiable. The division of Chinese history into periods (Spring and Autumn, Warring States, etc.) is a historiographic framework imposed by later scholars, not a contemporary categorization.

graph_edges.note: The Warring States period lasted until 221 BCE, when Qin unified China
Confucius's teachings became increasingly influential after his death, shaping Chinese civilization
Athenian naval expansion continued, eventually leading to the transformation of the Delian League into an empire
